title: GUIDELINES FOR THE CONTROL OF CAMPYLOBACTER AND SALMONELLA IN CHICKEN MEAT

sections.section: GUIDELINES FOR THE CONTROL OF CAMPYLOBACTER AND SALMONELLA IN CHICKEN MEAT
sections.summary: Document title only: Guidelines for the control of Campylobacter and Salmonella in chicken meat (no substantive provisions in this section).

Introduction: Campylobacteriosis and salmonellosis are major foodborne diseases with chicken meat an important vehicle; the Guidelines apply a risk management framework, build on Codex hygiene provisions and present GHP-based (qualitative) and hazard-based (quantitative) control measures, provide illustrative quantitative examples and a web-based decision tool, and are presented in a flow-diagram format to aid HACCP development, equivalence assessment and national flexibility.

sections.section: 5. PRINCIPLES APPLYING TO CONTROL OF CAMPYLOBACTER AND SALMONELLA IN CHICKEN MEAT
sections.summary: Principles: control of Campylobacter and Salmonella in chicken meat should incorporate food safety risk-analysis principles across the chain, and Competent Authorities should, where possible, set objective risk-management metrics to express required levels of pathogen control.

Risk profiles are an essential part of preliminary risk-management activities providing scientific information for designing tailored control systems, and these Guidelines are based on two 2007 broiler chicken risk profiles for Salmonella and Campylobacter.

sections.section: 7. PRIMARY PRODUCTION-TO-CONSUMPTION APPROACH TO CONTROL MEASURES
sections.summary: These Guidelines use a primary production-to-consumption flow-diagram approach to identify, evaluate and combine control measures at all food-chain steps, illustrating where GHP-based and hazard-based options apply and facilitating national and premises-level HACCP planning and flexibility.

sections.section: 7.1 Generic flow diagram for application of control measures
sections.summary: A generic primary production-to-consumption flow diagram is provided to guide identification of control measures and adaptation of HACCP plans, showing process steps from breeding flocks and hatchery through grower, slaughter, processing, chilling, packing and distribution to consumers.

sections.section: Process Flow Diagram 1: Primary production to Consumption
sections.summary: Process Flow Diagram 1 lists sequential process steps from grandparent and parent flocks, hatchery and grower operations, through transport, slaughter, dressing, chilling, portioning and packing, to storage, transport, retail/food service and the consumer, noting steps may vary by premises.

sections.section: ## 7.2. Availability of control measures at specific process flow steps addressed in these Guidelines
sections.summary: Explains that a table maps where GHP- and hazard-based control measures for Campylobacter and Salmonella have been identified across process flow steps, with ticks indicating presence and blanks indicating none; details are in these Guidelines or the OIE Terrestrial Animal Health Code and footnotes note that washing may occur at multiple dressing steps and provide a web reference.

Contains a list of production and supply-chain process steps (grandparent flocks through consumer) together with a control-measures table mapping GHP- and hazard-based measures for Campylobacter and Salmonella; the section includes table artifacts and image placeholders but no additional substantive requirements.

sections.section: 8. CONTROL MEASURES FOR STEPS 1 TO 11 (PRIMARY PRODUCTION)
sections.summary: States these primary production control guidelines supplement the OIE Terrestrial Animal Health Code and relevant CAC codes and then prescribes GHP-based measures (biosecurity, personnel hygiene, pest control) and Salmonella-specific measures including keeping breeder flocks Salmonella-free, following OIE response actions if positive, treating/storing/delivering feed to minimise Salmonella (preferably using dedicated vehicles), that some vaccines/additives may require competent-authority approval, separating and tracing contaminated eggs/chicks during hatchery and transport, and notes fly screens can markedly reduce Campylobacter prevalence.

For Salmonella: recommends hatching only eggs from Salmonella-negative flocks and, if contaminated eggs must be used, keeping and hatching them separately from other eggs with traceback to infected breeding flocks and review of control measures.

For Salmonella: requires breeder flocks be kept free of Salmonella and, if a flock is Salmonella-positive, that responses outlined in the OIE Terrestrial Animal Health Code Chapter 6.5 be implemented; additionally, breeder feed should be treated, stored and delivered to minimise Salmonella (preferably in dedicated vehicles) and use of vaccines, competitive exclusion products or certain additives may require competent-authority approval.
GHP-based measure: personnel transporting day-old chicks should not enter livestock buildings and must prevent cross-contamination during loading and unloading.

For Salmonella: only eggs from Salmonella-negative flocks should be sent for hatching, and when that is not practical eggs from Salmonella-positive flocks should be transported separately from other eggs.
For parent flocks, the same control measures required in Step 1 are explicitly applied at this step (i.e., obligations from Step 1 carry forward to parent flock management).

When eggs from Salmonella‑contaminated flocks must be used, they must be kept and hatched separately and resulting chicks isolated from other flocks, and contamination should be traced back to infected breeding flocks with control measures reviewed.

When using eggs from Salmonella‑contaminated flocks, eggs must be kept and hatched separately and chicks isolated, and authorities should perform traceback to infected breeding flocks and review control measures.

Personnel transporting day‑old chicks must not enter livestock buildings, must follow biosecurity procedures during loading/unloading to prevent cross‑contamination, and all live‑bird transport crates/modules should be cleaned, disinfected and dried before reuse.

Specific Salmonella control measures (e.g., competitive exclusion bacteria, organic acids in preslaughter drinking water, organic acids or formaldehyde in feed) may require approval from a competent authority before use.

Control of Campylobacter and Salmonella in flocks should combine biosecurity and personnel hygiene measures, with the particular national mix determined in consultation with stakeholders and pest control programmes designed to local conditions.
